one_line_takeaway: A privacy-motivated survey and 11-point roadmap for applying federated learning to student mental-health analysis, whose main value is identifying that FL is unexplored in education despite ready-made decentralized datasets - it proposes privacy defenses (DP, crypto, unlearning) as future work but implements and evaluates none.
key_finding: FL is an emerging but underused approach in mental-health research and is almost entirely unexplored in the specific intersection of mental health AND education: only a handful of FL-in-education studies exist (e.g., Tabassum et al. depression via PHQ-9 + smartphone sensors; Qirtas et al. loneliness via StudentLife). Crucially, several relevant datasets (Healthy Minds Study, National College Health Assessment, Add Health, StudentLife, Nurse Stress Wearables, ABCD, UK Biobank) are ALREADY inherently decentralized/multi-institution, so FL benchmarks could be built on them immediately with no new data collection - this is the paper's central short-term opportunity claim.
core_contribution: This is a survey and research roadmap (not an empirical paper) arguing for migrating mental-health analysis in education from centralized ML to federated learning (FL) to avoid transferring students' sensitive data to central servers. The authors' OWN contributions are four synthesis/positioning artifacts: (1) a review of AI/ML applications to student mental health organized by condition (stress, anxiety, depression, ADHD, SUD) and academic-performance prediction (Sec. III, Table I); (2) a review of existing FL-in-mental-health work with the explicit gap finding that FL is barely applied in EDUCATIONAL settings (Sec. IV, Table II); (3) a catalog of relevant datasets grouped into educational, general human-centered, and psychiatric domains, annotated with features/labels/ML-tasks/accessibility and whether each is inherently centralized vs. decentralized (Sec. V-A, Tables III-IV); and (4) a research roadmap splitting future work into a short-term vision (conventional FL over the already-decentralized datasets; merging heterogeneous datasets) and ten long-term visions: Vertical FL, complementary server-side learning, personalized/multi-task FL, LLMs-as-counselors within FL, XAI, multi-modal FL, federated unlearning, security/privacy (DP + cryptography), alternative FL architectures (decentralized/semi-decentralized/hierarchical), and FL under data/concept drift.
threat_model: The paper is motivated by concrete privacy risks but does NOT formalize an adversary or evaluate any attack or defense. Stated risks, all grounded in the text: centralized ML aggregates sensitive mental-health data at a server, exposing it to data breaches and unauthorized access (Sec. III-G); anonymization is insufficient because re-identification attacks can recover identities from 'anonymized' data (Sec. I-B); and even in FL, where raw data stays local, transmitted model updates can leak client data via model-inversion attacks (Sec. V-J). These motivate the paper's PROPOSED (not implemented) defenses/directions: differential privacy (noise on transmitted updates), cryptographic aggregation (homomorphic/functional encryption), federated unlearning to honor GDPR/CCPA 'right to be forgotten', and decentralized/semi-decentralized FL to remove the single-point-of-failure of a central aggregator. No adversary capabilities, no attack success rates, and no privacy-utility measurements are provided by the authors.
limitations: As a survey/roadmap it provides no empirical validation: no privacy-utility tradeoff numbers, no accuracy comparisons of its own, and no demonstration that any proposed direction (VFL across clinics+schools, DP-per-modality in MMFL, hierarchical personalized FL) actually works. The authors themselves note the enabling data does not yet exist for key directions - e.g., truly VERTICAL datasets linking the same students' clinical and educational records are not publicly available (Sec. V-C), so the most novel proposals are currently unrealizable. Concrete FL results cited (WESAD stress, DAIC-WOZ depression, StudentLife loneliness) are OTHER authors' work, not evidence produced here; the paper's contribution is synthesis and agenda-setting, not new method or measurement.

datasets_used: SURVEYED/CATALOGUED ONLY (no experiments run by the authors) - Education domain (Table III): Add Health, Australian Student Performance, Longitudinal Study of Australian Children, National College Health Assessment, Student-Depression-Text, StudentLife Study, Student Stress Factors, Healthy Minds Study, University Students Mental Health, 'What Makes a University Student Life Ideal?'

SURVEYED/CATALOGUED ONLY - General human-centered domain (Table IV): AnnoMI, CLPsych 2015, eRisk, Mental Health Support Feature Analysis (Reddit), National Comorbidity Survey, National Survey on Drug Use and Health, NLP Mental Health Conversations, Nurse Stress Prediction Wearable Sensors, Stress in America Survey, WESAD
SURVEYED/CATALOGUED ONLY - Psychiatric domain (Table IV): Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD), DAIC-WOZ, UK Biobank

Datasets appearing in CITED FL studies (not the authors' experiments): WESAD (FL stress, refs [52],[98]), DAIC-WOZ (FL speech depression, ref [102]), StudentLife (FL loneliness, ref [105]), PHQ-9 + smartphone sensor data (FL depression in students, ref [104])
quantitative_results: NONE. The paper reports no numerical results of its own - no accuracy, F1, privacy-budget (epsilon), or communication-cost figures. Tables I-IV are purely descriptive (references, data modality, objectives, features, labels, ML tasks, accessibility, collection mode) and contain no metrics.
baselines_compared: NONE run by the authors. The paper contrasts paradigms conceptually - centralized ML vs. federated learning - and notes qualitatively (from cited work, e.g., Fauzi et al. on WESAD) that FL typically trades some accuracy for privacy, but performs no head-to-head experimental comparison itself.
